sections.title: Chairman’s Chat
sections.content:
Martin Baxter’s Christmas Quiz

When it comes to club matters, are you a Father Christmas or a Scrooge?

  • I’m on the club committee.  
  • I’m a club coach.
  • I organise an event or competition.
  • I’ve completed a First Aid Course.
  • I carry a First Aid Kit.
  • I contribute to Skywords, the club magazine.
  • I know where the Horse & Farrier is.
  • I post on the Flying Plans WhatsApp group before I go flying.
  • I offer other pilots a lift/retrieve.
  • I submit a CANP if I’m flying during the working week.
  • I contribute to the club forum (or post constructive comments on our social media channels).
  • I’ve only recently qualified, but I intend to contribute when I have a little more experience.
  • I responded to the recent requests to vote for an award.
  • On 5 Dec I’ll be taking an interest at the AGM, and congratulating my fellow club members on the trophies that they have been awarded.
  • None of the above.  I’ve paid my membership fee and that gives me every right to fly in the Dales without making any other contribution.

subsections.title: AGM Notice 2024
subsections.content:
DHPC Annual General Meeting

December 5th 2024. 7.30 for 8 pm start

Horse and Farrier, Bridge St, Otley. LS21 1BQ

All members of the Dales Hang Gliding and Paragliding Club are invited to the AGM. Voting on committee proposals will only be possible in person at the meeting.

No proposals were received from members in response to the AGM notice in the October edition of Skywords.

AGENDA

1. Actions from previous AGM.

Action: Committee to seek quote for auditing of accounts from accountant in Hawes.

2. Adoption of reports from committee members.

Reports from committee members will be published on the website by the end of November and in the December edition of Skywords.

Members will be able to raise any questions regarding these at the AGM.

3. Approval of financial statement.

The Treasurer’s report for 2023/4 will be published on the website by 19th November, including proposed expenditure for 2024/5.

4. Appointment of Auditors.

See item 6.

5. Subscriptions 2024/5.

Committee proposal:

i. Membership fees remain the same (£25/£22.50 for prompt payment).

ii. Contributions to the Flying Fund to remain at 35% of subscription income.

iii. Until further notice, to adopt the HMRC approved mileage rates. For 2024/5 this

would raise the mileage rate from 40p/mile to 45p/mile.

6. Revision of Club Constitution/Rules.

Committee proposal:

Remove Rule 5a, subsection 4. “Appointment of auditors”,

Amend current rule 4f:

4f. The Club's financial year shall end on 30 September. Accounts shall be opened for inspection by any member of The Club at any reasonable time.

7. Election of Officers.

All committee posts are up for re-election. You are welcome to stand for any post. All you need is a little spare time and a willingness to put something back into the club. No current committee member is going to be insulted if you want to put your name forward.

8. AOB.

Not for voting on.

Mark Morrison (Tam)

Secretary

2nd November 2024

Please note:

Following the formal business of the AGM, the annual awards for 2024 will be made – the prized club trophies and XC mugs. Come along to receive recognition of your success and help celebrate other members' achievements! Awards will be handed out by Richard Meek, National XC League winner for the second year.

subsections.title: Chairman
subsections.content:
Committee

Just one casualty this year.  Simon Browning was elected as Sites Officer (South) at the last AGM but, faced with the ‘opportunity of a lifetime’, spent most of the year sailing around the coast of Britain.  After much soul searching, he’s decided to give up paragliding and will be standing down at this AGM.  I’m delighted to report that Stephen Craven has offered to stand in his place.

Not a committee post but Simon Tomlinson has also been lured away from paragliding (to sand yachting).  Liam Toothill has stepped up to take on the maintenance of our weather station.

In May Rob Urselmann was co-opted onto the committee to organise First Aid training within the club.  He has agreed to stand for the post at this AGM.

Farmers’ Dinner

Run as a stand-alone event with no prize giving this year, membership participation was poor.  Hosting was performed mostly by committee members who were rewarded with a free meal.

Other Responsibilities

I continue to sit on the BHPA Executive Committee as Sites Officer.  I also represent the club (and other local clubs and schools) at the Regional Airspace User Working Group – a 6 monthly meeting held at RAF Leeming.

subsections.title: Secretary
subsections.content: A frustrating year for many, certainly including me, with availability never seeming to match up with opportunity. Next season WILL be better! Others proved it was possible to get good days if you were determined enough. Another successful Fly-In / X Dales capped off another healthy year for the club though. Personally I was pleased the club membership voted to support the Kinga Masztalerz film to broaden the appeal of our sport. The low point of the year, or indeed any year I've been a member of the club, was undoubtedly Kev McLoughlin's untimely death in April - an horrendous shock to everyone. All Northern clubs were well represented at the funeral, bearing witness to his wide popularity and the respect in which he was held. RIP Kev.

subsections.title: Treasurer
subsections.content: We do not publish the Treasurer’s Report here because Skywords is an open source document. His report is published on the members only forum here.

subsections.title: Membership 2024
subsections.content:
The values in this report are aligned with the financial reporting period (01/10/23 to 30/09/24) and not the membership period (01/02/23 – 31/01/2024).

Membership Fees for the new year will remain the same [MB Edit:subject to agreement at the AGM]

·        Renewals with 10% Discount for early renewal = £22.50

·        New Joiners and Renewals after early discount period = £25

·        Half Year joining fee = £12.50

This year had 218 members which is slightly down from last year's figure of 222. As expected, most renewals are within January and February taking advantage of the discount. There was an increase in the number of members taking advantage of the discount from the previous year.

Rahul Basu.

Membership Secretary

subsections.title: Safety
subsections.content:
Safety Officer’s Report: 2024

All incidents by Dales Club members either in the UK or abroad should also be reported to the Dales Club Safety Officer, Clive Sury.  In this way any lessons learnt can be shared with our members.

There were 5 incidents reported either involving DHPC members or on DHPC sites in 2024.  In two of these incidents a key lesson is to call emergency services quickly and for both the injured person and others to be aware that injuries may require assistance.  Not to take the ‘I’m OK’ from the person involved in the incident at face value as adrenaline and other cultural factors may be overriding a significant need of care and help.

Please be reminded that all reports help others learn and provide guidance on safety matters with the potential to reduce injury for all of us.  Therefore, members are reminded and encouraged to report all incidents on the BHPA incident form here: https://contact.bhpa.co.uk/incident.php.  Plus, please send a copy of the report to Clive Sury for the club to benefit from any lessons learnt – email address:  safety@dhpc.org.uk

If people would like to know the details of each incident reported on our sites within the last year, then these and all others reported can be found on the BHPA incident report log here:  https://www.bhpa.co.uk/documents/safety/informal_investigations

Of note is the use of the term ‘incident’ by the BHPA in lieu of ‘accident’.  An incident is not just if someone is injured but also equipment failure or if an event could have resulted in an accident. The emphasis on regarding all these events as incidents is to encourage reporting of instances that could have led to more serious outcomes e.g., near misses, equipment failures or narrow escapes from injury.  In this way, by reporting such ‘incidents’ wider learning can be made, and hopefully more adverse outcomes avoided in the future.

Therefore, reiterating the point in the first paragraph, please submit incidents to both the Dales Safety Officer and BHPA when they occur. This helps yours and others safety knowledge.

Thank you to everyone who has submitted a report, and I wish you all safe flying for the next year.

2024 (5 incidents)

  •  May – Model Ridge
  • June – Nonts Sarah
  • July – Windbank
  • October - Ingleborough
  • October - Bir (India)

2025 Reserve Re-pack Day

booked for Saturday 18th January at Ilkley Grammar School.

Safe flying

Clive Sury, DHPC Safety Officer

subsections.title: Chief Coach
subsections.content:
Coaching changes

We currently have 21 coaches for a club of 218, a healthy number with a good geographic spread.

I was happy to add Rhian Ebrey, Joe Edmonds & Jason Perry who were endorsed Club Coaches.

It was tragic to lose Kev McLoughlin as both club member and a long served coach too. We're all thankful for the effort he put into the sport he loved.

Pete Balmforth stepped back from being a club coach too as he gave up flying. Thanks to him for his service as chief coach.

Clive Sury & Simon Browning stopped coaching too as their flying is not up to date. Thanks Clive & Simon; hopefully we'll see you return to the coaching list. Rhian's now moved to the lakes so her coaching transfers up to their team.

New Pilots

Rhian Ebrey, Paul Rowntree, Alan Oliver all gained pilot ratings this year.

Coaching Award

This year's coaching award goes to Joe Edmonds for the effort he's put into the dXCa/10-100 Club. Taking people out on several XC days. We had CPs doing first hops over he back and extended ridge runs. Appreciated Joe!

Coaching Activity

We had a coaching day on May 5th at Windbank. It wasn't very windy.

Martin Baxter and Chris Kay lead site familiarisation sessions on bad weather days at the Fly In. I lead gear discussion and harness setup with attendees there too. At 10-100 gatherings and regular flying days I, and other coaches have been conducting site briefings, giving XC advice and taking pilots through instrument and gear setup.

I created the DHPC Progress Telegram group to aid new CPs in getting together for flying, asking questions with coaches and generally feeling welcome to the club. It has 49 members, nine of whom are coaches or experienced pilots willing to help.

I have assisted Ian Currer & the BHPA in providing feedback about the online Pilot Exam version where it has been difficult for CPs with particular questions.

Next Year's Plans:

The Chief Coach can seem a bit admin oriented sometimes. I want to see if bringing some system to it can make it quicker to get through the comms etc. The website coaching section also needs an overhall which is on the winter todo list. We'll work out a way for Mike Barker to get his Paramotor Pilot Rating in the coming year. Alan Oliver is due to become a new club coach along with potentially Mike Barker and Jake Herbert too.

Pete Logan

subsections.title: Sites (North)
subsections.content:
My first full year as North Sites Officer, which I've really enjoyed. I'd always suspected that there was a lot more to keeping the club going than I'd realised, and it's been great to see this in action, and the time and effort being put in behind the scenes.

I think it's been a relatively quiet year in terms of our northern sites - no major issues or dramas. Thanks to my predecessors, there are well-established relationships with landowners, farmers, and others who live and work in the area. I'm grateful particularly to Simon T for being so organised, and handing over detailed and well-developed spreadsheets that I've heavily relied on.

A few things I've been involved with this year; updating some site guides to include Ed's Northern Challenge Trophy tasks, and Defined Flying Challenges; liaising with various gamekeepers (esp. Nappa and some land neighbouring established flying sites); ensuring (mostly Wensleydale) farmers were on the minibus to the Farmer's Dinner.

I'm just about to embark on the job of buying and distributing 40 or so bottles, plus some chocolates, biscuits etc for almost 40 households on our northern sites list for Christmas presents. I can't deny that it's a bit of a pain to sort out logistically in the run up to Christmas, but it's another of the small but vital things the club does in order to keep in touch with and generate goodwill from those who provide us with access to flying sites.

I'm sometimes struggling to balance the demands of work (in London each week), family commitments, and other things taking up my time, with the essential tasks for the sites. Thank you Martin for leaning in to help whenever possible - it's greatly appreciated!

Looking ahead, there's a few things I'd like to do in addition to the regular jobs, which tend to follow the seasons. Gamekeepers can represent some of our more difficult relationships, but I've made good progress in getting to know who covers which areas, letting them know about flying and how this does and doesn't impact their work, and this is something I'll continue to focus on.

Tom Shuttleworth

subsections.title: Sites (South)
subsections.content:
Addingham Moorside

 – not often flown but I believe there have been a few successful days.  

Baildon

 – Continues to be a great site for ground handling and other practice activities.  The Baildon Sod was held in September and it’s a great social event in the club calendar.  

Cow Close Fell

 – not often flown but great in the right conditions.  We don’t have regular contact with the farmer, so please be courteous and grateful if you meet them.  

Cowling & Sutton Pinnacles

– the landowners are regular attendees at the Farmers’ Dinner.  Not a popular site but let’s keep it open and fly it when we can.

Great Whernside

– effectively managed by a Commoners’ Association and we enjoy a good relationship with the farmer closest to the site.  It’s important to observe the Site Guide information and keep away from the Grouse Moor; failing to do so could make access more difficult in future.

Ilkley Moor

– flown a number of times this season.  I’ve trampled the bracken at the TO on several occasions during the years, and would ask others to do so if at the TO on a walk or to commit aviation.  It will make for a much friendlier TO in the future.

Kettlewell

 – we are trying to contact the tenant farmer for the village cricket field (just across the river) as this is generally regarded as a good landing option.  Ownership of the historic landing field has changed and we don’t have any contact.  Whilst not often flown, please be aware that there is no current understanding with the farmer.

Kilnsey

 – mind the trees!  Not the friendliest site, but it would be good to see it flown a few days a year to keep it as an active site.

Windbank & Hawkswick

 – Arguably the most important and most flown of the Southern sites.  Responsibility for the parking area at Windbank has changed and there may be a new payment method in the future.  In the meantime, please pay if requested!  There have also been changes to the Windbank TO ownership and whilst we expect things to remain the same for the club, please be aware that we need to demonstrate that we respect the land and stock.  Hawkswick is less used for access and TO, and there have been changes to the ownership there in recent years as well.

Other Matters

The Farmers Dinner 

proved very popular with the farmers this year.  It is a great way to thank the farmers for their support for our flying activities on their land and a good opportunity to reinforce the goodwill upon which we depend for our activities; it was good to welcome a good number of farmers from the Southern Sites.  The Booze Run is planned for mid-December together with the handing out of Christmas cards and a letter introducing the new Sites South Officer

New Officer

 - I’m grateful to Stephen Craven for offering to take on this role, of course anyone is welcome to stand should there be a rush of volunteers.

Questions/Info

 – please contact the Sites South Officer .

Simon Browning

Sites Officer (South)

subsections.title: Paragliding competitions
subsections.content:
- it’s not all about the numbers

It is official, Richard Meek is the winner of the national XC league 2024 making this the second year in a row; chapeau Richard!  Interestingly he won without a single circuit flight which is pretty unusual in the era of Tesco points.  In fact this was a quiet year in terms of circuit flights, both nationally and locally, probably a reflection on the weather as much as anything else.

In the Dales, Chris Kay crushed the competition once again with some excellent flights from a variety of different sites; it pays to get out frequently as well as having loads of ability!  Chris Fountain stormed into second spot with another 500km haul, whilst yours truly scraped over the line for the third spot and his first 500km total.

However, the real story is once again about the strength and depth of cross-country pilots in the Dales.  Ok we did not do as well in the national league this year, placing sixth, but with 36 pilots entering 208 flights and totalling more than 8700 points, it is fair to say we still have a very enthusiastic and broad cross-country pilot base.  That is despite some absences and top names having subdued years for various reasons.

On a personal note, this is the last year I will be “doing” the role of competition secretary.  It is not an onerous role, and has been immensely pleasurably handing out mugs at the end of the season, updating site records, compiling the list of trophy winners and trying to ignore the Chairman’s micro-management.  But this year mountain rescue commitments have finally meant I have not been able to even give enough time to attend committee meetings or write regular bulletins for Skywords.  So it is time for a change and for someone who has an interest in competitions to take up the baton.

So once again thank you to everyone for making the Dales such an amazing club, and massive congratulations for all who achieved personal bests, first 100kms or other bucket list flights this year.  The most enjoyable aspect of my time on the committee has undoubtedly been seeing the progression of new cross country pilots in the club.  It is now time to hand that pleasure on to someone else.

Prizes and mugs for the 2024 season to be dished out on Thursday 5th December at the Horse and Farrier in Otley.

Competition secretary

subsections.title: Hang gliding
subsections.content:
There were no recorded XC flights from the Dales this year, so no HG trophies will be awarded.

Trev has been in touch with the National HG Comps Panel.  They are aware that we have lifted the ban on comps at Semer Water.  They don't plan to run the BOS in the Dales in 2025, but may consider it for 2026.

subsections.title: Social Secretary
subsections.content:
There are two main areas of responsibility that fall to the Social Secretary; to organise a series of monthly winter social evenings from October to April and to help with the September Dales Fly In.

Winter Social Season

The winter season of monthly socials seems to continue to be well attended, despite the sometimes haphazard nature of its organisation. Often we have fantastic speakers of international import, like Jocky Sanderson and (our own) Richard Meek. Other times, we simply put questions to experienced pilots or have an auction of our surplus kit.

Either way, the socials bring together old members and new and are an excellent forum for sharing practice and maintaining our sorely needed parachat throughout the dark months.

Please continue to put the first Thursday of every month into your diaries between October and April. We have plenty to look forward to with speakers coming from the North Yorkshire Gliding Club and the latest thinking in SIV lessons next year.

Additionally, if you'd like to hear about a topic, would like to lend a hand or have an idea for an evening, please let me know.

Dales Fly In

The Dales Fly In this year was a further solidifying of the event on our annual calendar. It has firmly embedded the X Dales Hike and Fly into the Dales Fly In and cemented them as one of our nation's best-loved HnF events. Even when the wind is too strong, the sun may be out and there's plenty to do (and drink) in the fair dales resort of Settle.

subsections.title: Librarian
subsections.content:
The library spreadsheet has been updated to include the club's equipment and most recent purchase, the porosity meter. If/when equipment changes hands please let me know to keep the whereabouts of said items up to date.

Suggestions for new books are always welcome, we have a small budget to cover 2 or 3 new books per year and several books have been donated this year and are now on the spreadsheet...link on the website.

Cheers

Mike

Library@dhpc.org.uk

subsections.title: Newsletter
subsections.content:
We've nearly got things the way I would like them. Still a few more niggles to iron out.

The main strength of the newsletter is the really high standard of regular and occasional contributions. We're very lucky as a club.

Joseph Edmonds, Newsletter

subsections.title: Trophies
subsections.content:
Trophies were engraved by Green Slate Trophies (Burnley), the same place I've been using since I took up this post, costs were in line with budget forecast.

Sites and News

Martin Baxter reports

I was recently reminded about the accident that took place at Dunstable a few years ago.  A novice pilot attempted to top land in the take-off area when somebody inflated in front of him.  He blamed the coach and club for not telling him not to land there.  The claim was settled, by BHPA insurance, out of court.

The only Dales site that I can think of where we have rules for deconfliction is Wether Fell: we specify separate areas for hang gliders and paragliders (check the site guide and see below if you’re not sure).  If you’re giving a site briefing (coach or not) make sure that you include this, and try to stick to it even if there are no hang gliders (or paragliders) present: it reinforces the rule.  At other sites, without specific rules, responsibility for deconfliction rests entirely with individual pilots.

If low, then throw

Steve Gill reports

I was reading Rob's account of his accident in November's Skywords, There were a lot of similarities with an accident I had while out in Algo 4 weeks ago.

The Crash.

I'm not 100% sure that it happened exactly as I remembered, but it definitely started as a spin. I would say as the air was a bit funny (weak lift on one side and stronger sink on the other) I looked at my flight on SeeYou to try and analyse it. It was 10s between initial spin and end of flight. I was soaring about 30m below the top of the hill/mountain Sierra de Ligar  in Algodonales, Spain.

In the 10s I lost 52m. Peak was 7m/s. I reckon out of the 10s I tried to get the wing under control for 4s, another 4s where I should have thrown the reserve and the last 2s where it might have not been worth it.

The strange thing is that I had flown over the same area loads in the past. A few flew over ahead of me, and after I crashed a few flew over low to check if I was ok and had no problems at all with any turbulence.

The spin was so rapid though and went for normal flight to a full 360 in less than a second, with the lines twisted.

Seemed to be under control for a second then the wing started to spiral down. I did a full weight shift to the outside and applied outside brake. I did a couple of turns like this, then assessed that the bank angle wasn't decreasing, so it was only then that I thought to throw the reserve but realised it was too late. About a second before crashing I thought that this was going to be a nightmare getting the lines untangled from the small trees/bushes/scrub. About ½ second before the crash I saw the boulder through the trees/bushes that I was about to crash in to and thought "this is going to hurt" I unclipped from the glider and laid down for a minute to do an assessment, then when I tried to get out of the harness I realised that I had broken my leg.

I'd got myself into a comfortable position and someone flew over and shouted down if I was ok.. I stupidly replied "Yes I'm ok" I don't know why I did this, maybe through embarrassment or not wanting to put anyone to any trouble.

Graeme (Stephenson) had landed and phoned and asked where I was. I told him I had crashed and thought I had broken my leg. He asked if I was bleeding, to which I thought I would give an honest assessment and said yes, about a drop every 2 seconds. He replied " don't try to move" I think this must have panicked him a bit and he raced to get up the hill, stopping a car on the way.  I put a message on the Civa WhatsApp to say I needed help.

After this, it seemed like a very long wait, the bleeding had stopped.

Eventually after about an hour, I could hear shouting from 2 directions and knew people were close. Carlos arrived and then Graeme about 5 minutes later, his arms bleeding a bit and covered in scratches from the bushes. A paramedic and others arrived and put an inflatable splint on my leg. The pipe on the hand pump to inflate the splint didn't fit, so they tried to soften it with a lighter and open it out with scissors. Eventually sorted. Graeme and Jesus retrieved my wing and harness. Firemen had arrived and various people were cutting back branches to make a clearing. A helicopter and ambulance had arrived. The helicopter landed on the top of the hill as it didn't have a winch. The ambulance couldn't get near and the terrain made it too difficult to get a stretcher in. A police helicopter then arrived with a winch and I was given an injection to sedate me to be winched off to the waiting ambulance. The next thing I remember was being moved from the ambulance to the air ambulance on top of the hill which took me to Jerez hospital.

I spent the next 4 days there and it felt like 10. I got back home 2 days later and I'm slowly getting better.

My glider fared much better with no damage to the wing and only 2 upper cascade lines needing replacing.

My big take from the experience is "If low, then throw" Don't hesitate and think "I can get this under control".

Reflections

Rosie Darwood reports

“I’ve had an accident; I think I’ve broken my back”

“Rosie, do you copy, are you OK”

“Pete, copy, no, I’ve had an accident I think I’ve broken my back”

Severe pain and muscle spasm radiated across my lower back making it impossible to move.  My tongue detected a loose molar but everything else seemed OK.  Reassuringly I could move and feel my legs and feet.  I looked across at my wing, lying limp on the hillside but decided best to unclip from my harness anyway.  Gingerly I prodded my lumbar spine.  No bony tenderness.  Maybe it wasn’t broken?  I looked across to where we had been planning to camp, it was beautiful.  Retrieving my phone from my coat pocket I took a skew-whiff photo from my supine position then lay in the warm sunshine waiting for help.

Growing up at the bottom of the Long Mynd in Shropshire I had always wanted to fly.  I finally learnt in Manila, Australia in 2013, Godfrey persuading my husband Pete who had given me lessons as a surprise 40th birthday present that he should learn too.  An inflexible fulltime job (plus on-call) limits my UK flying hours, but we take holidays flying abroad to compensate with this being our fifth time in Bir.  Like other pilots I struggle with the environmental impact of long-haul flights, so we had more recently been restricting ourselves to one long-haul flight a year, this year was an exception.

I fly a Rush 6, a replacement for my tired Rush 5 after a trip to Colombia in January.  I have 800 hours, over 100 this year prior to the accident.  However, I had not flown much since Colombia and prior to going to Bir was concerned about my currency.  I planned to take it slowly and particularly to be cautious about “going over the back”.  I would describe myself as a cautious pilot.

The conditions in Bir were unusual this year.  We arrived on 13th October and cloud was forming early each day with a relatively low base.  We stayed on the front ridge, gradually building up and gaining confidence again.  By the day of the accident, I had done 40 hours and was feeling comfortable on my wing and in the conditions.  We were halfway through the holiday had taken a short sortie over the back one day but not yet camped out this year which we were keen to do.  The plan that day was to climb out and head over the back if possible and camp on Parashar ridge.  If that wasn’t possible, we would camp on Hobbiton.

It was a great day.  We climbed out efficiently and met above Hanuman Garh.  We left for the glide over the back at 3500m, changing to thick gloves on route and arrived above the ridge, fantastic, no scrabbling required.  We headed up the ridge to Barot Peak.  Conditions were strong but OK.  Pete pushed further back towards the glacier but I was apprehensive, so we instead crossed the Lohardi valley to fly past Dainsar lake and Sari pass.  Conditions here were less pleasant.  I think there was a north-easterly forecast high up so wonder if we were effectively flying a bit in the lee.  We passed Sari pass reaching the end of Parashar ridge and started to head down the ridge.  At the next thermal I took a big collapse.  “I think I’ve had enough Pete, it seems a shame to waste this height (4500m) but I want to land or head back”.  The front ridge looked tempting; I could pretty much glide there from here and then relax.

Passing Dehnasar Lake

Parasher Ridge

“Ok, you find somewhere to land and I will come and join you”.

People were landing on the rises on Parashar ridge which was very thermic.  Between the rises was a shallow col with smoother air, so mistakenly I thought this might be a better place to land.  I gradually lost height.  Although the conditions were very thermic the slope in front of the ridge into the valley was tree covered with no obvious landing options, so I also didn’t want to end up too low. After what seemed an age of descent punctuated by lift, I finally got to within striking distance of landing.  However, my last turn inadvertently took me slightly behind the front of the ridge which also had a lip I hadn’t appreciated from above.  The response of the wing to the rotor was swift; a sharp collapse 2-3m above the ground and I landed hard, I think on my bottom.

There appear to have been a lot of accidents in Bir this year and part of the reason for this reflection was to see if analysis of my multiple errors might be of assistance to anyone else.  I have identified three key factors:

1.     Choice of top landing (at all).

When we were learning to fly in Manila an experienced pilot died on Mount Borah trying to top land in thermic conditions.  If flying in the conditions over the back that day was challenging, top landing was also going to be challenging and maybe beyond my skillset.  It was approximately 1pm when I “landed”, i.e. right in the middle of the day.  I discovered later other more experienced pilots also had struggled to land and another experienced pilot also broke his spine further along the ridge from where I landed.  The correct decision after the collapse would have been to fly back to the front and land in the landing field.  I think this was the most important mistake.

2.     Choice of top landing site

The place I picked was NOT a good location to top-land.  The winds would have been stronger at the col and the lip at the front of the ridge created rotor.  I think this mistake was a reflection of my currency.  I have always thought of currency being primarily about wing handling and control and having been flying for 40 hours in Bir I do not think these aspects were a major issue.  But I now think currency is more than that.  It is about decision-making as well.  It was my first attempt at top landing of the holiday and the lack of currency was reflected in the incorrect site choice and allowing myself to be pushed back.  Although I had practised top-landing at home in the Dales this is very different.

3.     Plan continuation bias

We love vol-biv.  We rarely have a precise plan, that day was a bit of an exception.  Plan continuation bias is when we continue to follow a plan despite the evidence suggesting an alternative plan would be preferable.  Things such as the scarcity of “really good days” up to that point will feed into that, not wanting to miss an opportunity and pushing harder than you should or taking risks.  The answer is to “fly the conditions”.  I don’t know how much other people’s flights fuel this mindset too; other people had been camping out so we felt we should be able to. XContest can be a source of inspiration but can also be a source of danger.

When we first went to Bir an experienced “Bir hand” sat us down and talked to us about flying there.  “It is different to the UK.  People die.  There is a death every year.  You need to fly within your ability”.

Two pilots were with me already by the time I saw Pete running down the slope towards me.  Moving was painful but eventually they managed to shuffle me by degrees down the slope to a flatter area by the lake.  Carefully I managed to sit up and then later take a few steps with my walking poles.  We discussed rescue options.  There was a trekking route nearby and a ground rescue would probably arrive sooner than a helicopter which wouldn’t arrive until the following day.  However, initiating the rescue via Inreach it rapidly became clear there would be no rescue of any sort until the following day.  We had camping kit, food and pain relief, so Pete prioritised cooking and getting me into a shelter before the sun went down and the temperature dropped.  The night was not actually too bad.  Prodding my back again in the morning however I located the bony tenderness, higher up than I had checked before.  With help I extricated myself from the tent and lay on my Thermarest in the sun, watching pilots climbing high on the ridge behind whilst we awaited developments.

Friends back in Bir had sensibly decided a helicopter was the best option.  There were however now three of us to rescue, another pilot further along the ridge also with a back injury but in more pain than I, and someone at 5000m whose condition was unknown.  As the day wore on it became apparent that I was, appropriately, the lowest priority of the three and mentally I prepared for another night on the hill.  Mid-afternoon the welcome sound of helicopter blades nearby got us to our feet.  The tent was packed and everything safely stowed but it flew past.  Wondering if perhaps it couldn’t land where we were I took the walking poles and slowly zigzagged up to the top of the next rise.  Soon they were back, landing really close.  The helicopters don’t have stretchers (there is only one helicopter with a stretcher facility in India) but the journey was only about 7-8 minutes down to Kullu hospital.

Awaiting Rescue

Helicopter Evacuation

I am immensely grateful to my friends (both in Bir and across the world) and those people who didn’t even know me who facilitated the rescue at personal cost, in terms of time, money and missed flying opportunities. Helicopters will not launch in Bir without some kind of up-front payment.  I am truly humbled by their generosity and proud to be part of such a supportive and truly international paragliding community. I am also incredibly fortunate to have  amazing siblings in the UK who tirelessly liaised with the insurance company.

From the rescue perspective there are a few relevant “essentials” for Bir which have been described elsewhere but may be worth repeating:

1.     An Inreach – or some kind of locator device and satellite communication device (notwithstanding the fact these are currently illegal in India).  I did not actually use the “SOS” button but did use the messaging extensively.  Whilst we had access to the radio safety channel the Inreach was far more useful.  We had no mobile phone reception but pairing the Inreach with your phone facilitates messaging (or should do; Pete’s unpaired and would not re-pair without a network connection).

2.     Camping kit and food – it is not uncommon to need to wait 24-48 hours for a rescue.

3.     Share insurance details with someone on the ground.

4.     It would also have been a very different experience with uncertain outcome if I had landed alone.

I had an unstable burst fracture of T12 (thoracic spine) which required fixation in India before repatriation and a broken tooth (which in due course gave me more pain than the spine!) which was thankfully removed at the same time.  The surgeon and all the staff at Fortis Hospital in Chandigarh were truly caring.  The long-term prognosis is good, although the minimum six months off impact exercise is going to prove tiresome.

Despite everything that has happened Bir is an amazing place and I will go back!  I hope that sharing my story may be useful to other pilots.

PG Tips 🫖 (Paragliding tips)

Stephen Craven reports

I am recovering from a Hernia operation at the moment so am unable to get out and take demonstration photos etc

So here is my tip from the couch.

Try to get in at least a few ground handling sessions over the winter period.

One session may be in very light wind to practice forward launching, because after a long walk up to go flying, you may find very little wind at TO (take off) and be faced with a scary launch from a cliff, or a long walk back down. Practising forward launching will give you more confidence and make you safer.

Even when just ground handling, go over your checks. It’s good to get into the habit, as there are often plenty of distractions when taking off. It should be automatic.

sections.title: Sites and News
sections.content:
Martin Baxter reports

I was recently reminded about the accident that took place at Dunstable a few years ago.  A novice pilot attempted to top land in the take-off area when somebody inflated in front of him.  He blamed the coach and club for not telling him not to land there.  The claim was settled, by BHPA insurance, out of court.

The only Dales site that I can think of where we have rules for deconfliction is Wether Fell: we specify separate areas for hang gliders and paragliders (check the site guide and see below if you’re not sure).  If you’re giving a site briefing (coach or not) make sure that you include this, and try to stick to it even if there are no hang gliders (or paragliders) present: it reinforces the rule.  At other sites, without specific rules, responsibility for deconfliction rests entirely with individual pilots.

sections.title: If low, then throw
sections.content:
Steve Gill reports

I was reading Rob's account of his accident in November's Skywords, There were a lot of similarities with an accident I had while out in Algo 4 weeks ago.

The Crash.

I'm not 100% sure that it happened exactly as I remembered, but it definitely started as a spin. I would say as the air was a bit funny (weak lift on one side and stronger sink on the other) I looked at my flight on SeeYou to try and analyse it. It was 10s between initial spin and end of flight. I was soaring about 30m below the top of the hill/mountain Sierra de Ligar  in Algodonales, Spain.

In the 10s I lost 52m. Peak was 7m/s. I reckon out of the 10s I tried to get the wing under control for 4s, another 4s where I should have thrown the reserve and the last 2s where it might have not been worth it.

The strange thing is that I had flown over the same area loads in the past. A few flew over ahead of me, and after I crashed a few flew over low to check if I was ok and had no problems at all with any turbulence.

The spin was so rapid though and went for normal flight to a full 360 in less than a second, with the lines twisted.

Seemed to be under control for a second then the wing started to spiral down. I did a full weight shift to the outside and applied outside brake. I did a couple of turns like this, then assessed that the bank angle wasn't decreasing, so it was only then that I thought to throw the reserve but realised it was too late. About a second before crashing I thought that this was going to be a nightmare getting the lines untangled from the small trees/bushes/scrub. About ½ second before the crash I saw the boulder through the trees/bushes that I was about to crash in to and thought "this is going to hurt" I unclipped from the glider and laid down for a minute to do an assessment, then when I tried to get out of the harness I realised that I had broken my leg.

I'd got myself into a comfortable position and someone flew over and shouted down if I was ok.. I stupidly replied "Yes I'm ok" I don't know why I did this, maybe through embarrassment or not wanting to put anyone to any trouble.

Graeme (Stephenson) had landed and phoned and asked where I was. I told him I had crashed and thought I had broken my leg. He asked if I was bleeding, to which I thought I would give an honest assessment and said yes, about a drop every 2 seconds. He replied " don't try to move" I think this must have panicked him a bit and he raced to get up the hill, stopping a car on the way.  I put a message on the Civa WhatsApp to say I needed help.

After this, it seemed like a very long wait, the bleeding had stopped.

Eventually after about an hour, I could hear shouting from 2 directions and knew people were close. Carlos arrived and then Graeme about 5 minutes later, his arms bleeding a bit and covered in scratches from the bushes. A paramedic and others arrived and put an inflatable splint on my leg. The pipe on the hand pump to inflate the splint didn't fit, so they tried to soften it with a lighter and open it out with scissors. Eventually sorted. Graeme and Jesus retrieved my wing and harness. Firemen had arrived and various people were cutting back branches to make a clearing. A helicopter and ambulance had arrived. The helicopter landed on the top of the hill as it didn't have a winch. The ambulance couldn't get near and the terrain made it too difficult to get a stretcher in. A police helicopter then arrived with a winch and I was given an injection to sedate me to be winched off to the waiting ambulance. The next thing I remember was being moved from the ambulance to the air ambulance on top of the hill which took me to Jerez hospital.

I spent the next 4 days there and it felt like 10. I got back home 2 days later and I'm slowly getting better.

My glider fared much better with no damage to the wing and only 2 upper cascade lines needing replacing.

My big take from the experience is "If low, then throw" Don't hesitate and think "I can get this under control".

sections.title: Reflections
sections.content:
Rosie Darwood reports

“I’ve had an accident; I think I’ve broken my back”

“Rosie, do you copy, are you OK”

“Pete, copy, no, I’ve had an accident I think I’ve broken my back”

Severe pain and muscle spasm radiated across my lower back making it impossible to move.  My tongue detected a loose molar but everything else seemed OK.  Reassuringly I could move and feel my legs and feet.  I looked across at my wing, lying limp on the hillside but decided best to unclip from my harness anyway.  Gingerly I prodded my lumbar spine.  No bony tenderness.  Maybe it wasn’t broken?  I looked across to where we had been planning to camp, it was beautiful.  Retrieving my phone from my coat pocket I took a skew-whiff photo from my supine position then lay in the warm sunshine waiting for help.

Growing up at the bottom of the Long Mynd in Shropshire I had always wanted to fly.  I finally learnt in Manila, Australia in 2013, Godfrey persuading my husband Pete who had given me lessons as a surprise 40th birthday present that he should learn too.  An inflexible fulltime job (plus on-call) limits my UK flying hours, but we take holidays flying abroad to compensate with this being our fifth time in Bir.  Like other pilots I struggle with the environmental impact of long-haul flights, so we had more recently been restricting ourselves to one long-haul flight a year, this year was an exception.

I fly a Rush 6, a replacement for my tired Rush 5 after a trip to Colombia in January.  I have 800 hours, over 100 this year prior to the accident.  However, I had not flown much since Colombia and prior to going to Bir was concerned about my currency.  I planned to take it slowly and particularly to be cautious about “going over the back”.  I would describe myself as a cautious pilot.

The conditions in Bir were unusual this year.  We arrived on 13th October and cloud was forming early each day with a relatively low base.  We stayed on the front ridge, gradually building up and gaining confidence again.  By the day of the accident, I had done 40 hours and was feeling comfortable on my wing and in the conditions.  We were halfway through the holiday had taken a short sortie over the back one day but not yet camped out this year which we were keen to do.  The plan that day was to climb out and head over the back if possible and camp on Parashar ridge.  If that wasn’t possible, we would camp on Hobbiton.

It was a great day.  We climbed out efficiently and met above Hanuman Garh.  We left for the glide over the back at 3500m, changing to thick gloves on route and arrived above the ridge, fantastic, no scrabbling required.  We headed up the ridge to Barot Peak.  Conditions were strong but OK.  Pete pushed further back towards the glacier but I was apprehensive, so we instead crossed the Lohardi valley to fly past Dainsar lake and Sari pass.  Conditions here were less pleasant.  I think there was a north-easterly forecast high up so wonder if we were effectively flying a bit in the lee.  We passed Sari pass reaching the end of Parashar ridge and started to head down the ridge.  At the next thermal I took a big collapse.  “I think I’ve had enough Pete, it seems a shame to waste this height (4500m) but I want to land or head back”.  The front ridge looked tempting; I could pretty much glide there from here and then relax.

Passing Dehnasar Lake

Parasher Ridge

“Ok, you find somewhere to land and I will come and join you”.

People were landing on the rises on Parashar ridge which was very thermic.  Between the rises was a shallow col with smoother air, so mistakenly I thought this might be a better place to land.  I gradually lost height.  Although the conditions were very thermic the slope in front of the ridge into the valley was tree covered with no obvious landing options, so I also didn’t want to end up too low. After what seemed an age of descent punctuated by lift, I finally got to within striking distance of landing.  However, my last turn inadvertently took me slightly behind the front of the ridge which also had a lip I hadn’t appreciated from above.  The response of the wing to the rotor was swift; a sharp collapse 2-3m above the ground and I landed hard, I think on my bottom.

There appear to have been a lot of accidents in Bir this year and part of the reason for this reflection was to see if analysis of my multiple errors might be of assistance to anyone else.  I have identified three key factors:

1.     Choice of top landing (at all).

When we were learning to fly in Manila an experienced pilot died on Mount Borah trying to top land in thermic conditions.  If flying in the conditions over the back that day was challenging, top landing was also going to be challenging and maybe beyond my skillset.  It was approximately 1pm when I “landed”, i.e. right in the middle of the day.  I discovered later other more experienced pilots also had struggled to land and another experienced pilot also broke his spine further along the ridge from where I landed.  The correct decision after the collapse would have been to fly back to the front and land in the landing field.  I think this was the most important mistake.

2.     Choice of top landing site

The place I picked was NOT a good location to top-land.  The winds would have been stronger at the col and the lip at the front of the ridge created rotor.  I think this mistake was a reflection of my currency.  I have always thought of currency being primarily about wing handling and control and having been flying for 40 hours in Bir I do not think these aspects were a major issue.  But I now think currency is more than that.  It is about decision-making as well.  It was my first attempt at top landing of the holiday and the lack of currency was reflected in the incorrect site choice and allowing myself to be pushed back.  Although I had practised top-landing at home in the Dales this is very different.

3.     Plan continuation bias

We love vol-biv.  We rarely have a precise plan, that day was a bit of an exception.  Plan continuation bias is when we continue to follow a plan despite the evidence suggesting an alternative plan would be preferable.  Things such as the scarcity of “really good days” up to that point will feed into that, not wanting to miss an opportunity and pushing harder than you should or taking risks.  The answer is to “fly the conditions”.  I don’t know how much other people’s flights fuel this mindset too; other people had been camping out so we felt we should be able to. XContest can be a source of inspiration but can also be a source of danger.

When we first went to Bir an experienced “Bir hand” sat us down and talked to us about flying there.  “It is different to the UK.  People die.  There is a death every year.  You need to fly within your ability”.

Two pilots were with me already by the time I saw Pete running down the slope towards me.  Moving was painful but eventually they managed to shuffle me by degrees down the slope to a flatter area by the lake.  Carefully I managed to sit up and then later take a few steps with my walking poles.  We discussed rescue options.  There was a trekking route nearby and a ground rescue would probably arrive sooner than a helicopter which wouldn’t arrive until the following day.  However, initiating the rescue via Inreach it rapidly became clear there would be no rescue of any sort until the following day.  We had camping kit, food and pain relief, so Pete prioritised cooking and getting me into a shelter before the sun went down and the temperature dropped.  The night was not actually too bad.  Prodding my back again in the morning however I located the bony tenderness, higher up than I had checked before.  With help I extricated myself from the tent and lay on my Thermarest in the sun, watching pilots climbing high on the ridge behind whilst we awaited developments.

Friends back in Bir had sensibly decided a helicopter was the best option.  There were however now three of us to rescue, another pilot further along the ridge also with a back injury but in more pain than I, and someone at 5000m whose condition was unknown.  As the day wore on it became apparent that I was, appropriately, the lowest priority of the three and mentally I prepared for another night on the hill.  Mid-afternoon the welcome sound of helicopter blades nearby got us to our feet.  The tent was packed and everything safely stowed but it flew past.  Wondering if perhaps it couldn’t land where we were I took the walking poles and slowly zigzagged up to the top of the next rise.  Soon they were back, landing really close.  The helicopters don’t have stretchers (there is only one helicopter with a stretcher facility in India) but the journey was only about 7-8 minutes down to Kullu hospital.

Awaiting Rescue

Helicopter Evacuation

I am immensely grateful to my friends (both in Bir and across the world) and those people who didn’t even know me who facilitated the rescue at personal cost, in terms of time, money and missed flying opportunities. Helicopters will not launch in Bir without some kind of up-front payment.  I am truly humbled by their generosity and proud to be part of such a supportive and truly international paragliding community. I am also incredibly fortunate to have  amazing siblings in the UK who tirelessly liaised with the insurance company.

From the rescue perspective there are a few relevant “essentials” for Bir which have been described elsewhere but may be worth repeating:

1.     An Inreach – or some kind of locator device and satellite communication device (notwithstanding the fact these are currently illegal in India).  I did not actually use the “SOS” button but did use the messaging extensively.  Whilst we had access to the radio safety channel the Inreach was far more useful.  We had no mobile phone reception but pairing the Inreach with your phone facilitates messaging (or should do; Pete’s unpaired and would not re-pair without a network connection).

2.     Camping kit and food – it is not uncommon to need to wait 24-48 hours for a rescue.

3.     Share insurance details with someone on the ground.

4.     It would also have been a very different experience with uncertain outcome if I had landed alone.

I had an unstable burst fracture of T12 (thoracic spine) which required fixation in India before repatriation and a broken tooth (which in due course gave me more pain than the spine!) which was thankfully removed at the same time.  The surgeon and all the staff at Fortis Hospital in Chandigarh were truly caring.  The long-term prognosis is good, although the minimum six months off impact exercise is going to prove tiresome.

Despite everything that has happened Bir is an amazing place and I will go back!  I hope that sharing my story may be useful to other pilots.

sections.title: PG Tips 🫖 (Paragliding tips)
sections.content:
Stephen Craven reports

I am recovering from a Hernia operation at the moment so am unable to get out and take demonstration photos etc

So here is my tip from the couch.

Try to get in at least a few ground handling sessions over the winter period.

One session may be in very light wind to practice forward launching, because after a long walk up to go flying, you may find very little wind at TO (take off) and be faced with a scary launch from a cliff, or a long walk back down. Practising forward launching will give you more confidence and make you safer.

Even when just ground handling, go over your checks. It’s good to get into the habit, as there are often plenty of distractions when taking off. It should be automatic.
